coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on calix[4]arene functionalized polymer dots for bisphenol A detection in real water samples
coredata.dc:creator: paydar, Sardar
Feizi, Foroozan
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Barati, Ali
Mousavi, Farimah
Matt, Dominique
coredata.dc:description: Bisphenol A (BPA) is one of key raw materials used in the production of epoxy resins and plastics, which has toxicological effects on humans by disrupting cell functions through a variety of cell signaling pathways. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a simple, rapid, and accurate BPA detection method in real water samples. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence method based on yellow-emitting surface-functionalized polymer dots (PFBT@L Pdots) and blue-emitting carbon dots (Cdots) was described for the detection of BPA. Pdots as the detecting part were synthesized by using highly fluorescent hydrophobic Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) polymer and (R)-5,11,17,23-Tetra-tert-butyl-25,27-bis[(diphenylphosphinoyl)methoxy]-26-(3-oxabutyloxy)-28-[(1-phenylethyl)- carbamoylmethoxy]calix [4]arene (L) functionalizing ligand, and Cdots as internal reference were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea. In the presence of BPA, chemical binding of the phosphorus atoms of nearby PFBT@L Pdots with BPA hydroxyl functional groups led to the aggregation of the PFBT@L Pdots aggregation and quenching their yellow emission, but the blue emission of Cdots, on the other hand, remained stable. The proposed PFBT@L Pdots probe was successfully applied for the detection of BPA in real water samples, and the results were in good agreement with those obtained by HPLC-FLD. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report that the calixarene has been utilized to modify Pdots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Carbon dots
Calixarenes
Fluorescence probe
Bisphenol A
Ratiometric method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid
Detecting BPA in real water sample
Design of probe and material characterization
Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of the BPA detection
BPA detection in the real water sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Research Council of Razi University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic structure of (a) BPA. (b) PFBT. (c) L.

Characterization of the PFBT@L Pdots. (a) Absorbance spectra of the PFBT, L, and PFBT@L Pdots. (b) The emission fluorescence spectra of the PFBT@L Pdots with different excitation from 250 nm to 430 nm. (c) TEM image of PFBT@L Pdots. (d) FT-IR spectra of L in the presence of BPA, L, PFBT@L Pdots, and PFBT@L Pdots in the presence of BPA.

Fluorescence quenching percentage ratios (F542/F440) of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid upon the addition of 1000 nM of BPA at different (a) pH values ranging from 4.0 to 9.0. (b) incubation time. (c) amount of L to synthesis of PFBT@L Pdots. (d) amount of PFBT@L Pdots for the detection a BPA.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid (11 μg mL−1) upon the addition of 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000, 1100, 1300, 1500, 1700, and 2000 nM of BPA, respectively. (b) The ratiometric fluorescence intensities (F542/F440) of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid vs [BPA]. F0 and F are ratio signals (F542/F440) in the absence and presence of BPA, respectively. (c) The fluorescence spectra of individual PFBT@L Pdots solution (8 μg mL−1) upon the addition of similar concentrations of BPA. (d) The stern-volmer plot of single fluorescence methods. Inset a and c: The concentrations of BPA from left to right are 0, 500, 1000, 1500, and 2000 nM, respectively. The fluorescence photos were taken under a UV lamp (excitation wavelength at 365 nm).
Selectivity of PFBT@L Pdots (8 μg mL−1) assay toward various interference.
The optimized structures of (a) L, (b) BPA, (c) L/BPA, (d) L-BPA, (e) BPA-L-BPA, (f) L-(BPA)2-L, and the corresponding molecular orbital energy diagrams.
The computational results of the QM study on the geometrically optimized L in different interaction modes with BPA.
Determination of BPA spiked in real water sample (n = 5).

row.entry: Mean found ± SD(nM) PFBT@L Probe HPLC

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on calix[4]arene functionalized polymer dots for bisphenol A detection in real water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Molecular Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory, Louis Pasteur University, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Molecular Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory, Louis Pasteur University, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is one of key raw materials used in the production of epoxy resins and plastics, which has toxicological effects on humans by disrupting cell functions through a variety of cell signaling pathways. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a simple, rapid, and accurate BPA detection method in real water samples. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence method based on yellow-emitting surface-functionalized polymer dots (PFBT@L Pdots) and blue-emitting carbon dots (Cdots) was described for the detection of BPA. Pdots as the detecting part were synthesized by using highly fluorescent hydrophobic Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) polymer and (R)-5,11,17,23-Tetra-tert-butyl-25,27-bis[(diphenylphosphinoyl)methoxy]-26-(3-oxabutyloxy)-28-[(1-phenylethyl)- carbamoylmethoxy]calix [4]arene (L) functionalizing ligand, and Cdots as internal reference were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea. In the presence of BPA, chemical binding of the phosphorus atoms of nearby PFBT@L Pdots with BPA hydroxyl functional groups led to the aggregation of the PFBT@L Pdots aggregation and quenching their yellow emission, but the blue emission of Cdots, on the other hand, remained stable. The proposed PFBT@L Pdots probe was successfully applied for the detection of BPA in real water samples, and the results were in good agreement with those obtained by HPLC-FLD. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report that the calixarene has been utilized to modify Pdots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenols are endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) and are widely used in a variety of industrial processes, including the production of plastics and epoxy resins [1,2]. Numerous studies show that three of the eighteen bisphenol chemical compounds, including Bisphenol A (BPA), Bisphenol S (BPS), and Bisphenol F (BPF) are found in humans. BPA (4,4′-isopropylidenediphenol) is a synthetic organic compound composed of two phenol rings connected by a methyl bridge with two methyl functional groups appended to the bridge (Fig. 1 a) [3] that was first synthesized by Aleksandr P. Dianin in 1891, and was soon found in both humans and the environment [1]. From when Sharp. L, in 1993, discovered that BPA is released from polycarbonate bottles at high temperature and pressure [4], investigation of BPA toxicity attracted much attention. Humans can be exposed to BPA through different routes, but food is the most common way, since BPA is a precursor monomer used to produce food packaging materials and drinking water plastic bottles. Therefore, it may release into the package's contents and endanger human health [5–9]. To be noted, BPA has toxicological effects on humans by disrupting cell function through a variety of cell signaling pathways. Studies show that the level of BPA in the urine is associated with increased risk of diabetes, impaired reproductive capacity, and cardiovascular disease [10]. Traditional methods used to detect BPA, such as conventional chromatography methods (HPLC, UHPLC–MS/MS, GC, and GC-IT/MS chromatography) are expensive and require complicated and time-consuming sample pretreatment procedures [11–13]. As a result, it is essential to develop rapid and simple BPA detection methods. Fluorescence spectroscopy is a popular and high sensitive method that can be a good candidate technique for this purpose. To date, a number of fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs), including carbon dots (Cdots), quantum dots (Qdots), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and polymer NPs have been utilized as fluorescent probes for BPA detection [14–18].

As a novel class of fluorescent NPs, semiconducting polymer dots (Pdots) have been received much attention since the first reports in 1980 [19]. These NPs, are composed of hydrophobic semiconducting polymers with at least 50 % (preferably higher than 80–90 %) weight concentration or volume fraction [20–23]. It is widely recognized that Pdots possess the desirable properties of fluorescence brightness, biocompatibility, and ease of surface functionalization. Generally, Pdots are functionalized using one of three methods: (1) encapsulation (2) direct functionalization, or (3) amphiphilic polymer coprecipitation [19]. The most common of these methods is coprecipitation, in which the fluorescent hydrophobic polymer is coprecipitated with a small amount of appropriate co-polymer or ligand with diverse functional groups [23–25].

Calix[n]arenes are versatile phenol-based macrocyclic molecules that are locked in the cone conformation. In these structures, lower-rim arms refer to the hydroxylic rim, and upper-rim arms are defined as hydrophobic rim [26–28]. Since the first report of calix[n]arenes in the 1970s, these macrocyclic molecules have been widely used for the preparation of multifunctional ligands, owing to their remarkable properties such as facile synthesis, variable size of the cavity, and simplicity in functionalizing both their lower and upper rims [29,30]. With these properties in mind, we decided to incorporate calixarene into the preparation of Pdots for the first time.

In the present work, we designed a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on PFBT@L Pdots/citric acid (CA) Cdots hybrid for BPA detection. The PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid is prepared by mixing Pdots as a sensing probe and CA Cdots as an internal standard. The yellow-emitting PFBT@L Pdots were synthesized by using high brightness Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) polymer as a fluorescent hydrophobic polymer and (R)-5,11,17,23-Tetra-tert-butyl-25,27-bis[(diphenylphosphinoyl)methoxy]-26-(3-oxabutyloxy)-28-[(1-phenylethyl)- carbamoylmethoxy] calix [4]arene (L) as an appropriate ligand for surface functionalization of Pdots, and blue-emitting Cdots were synthesized through simple hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea. Upon exposure to BPA, the PFBT@L Pdots yellow emission of Pdots was quenched, but the blue emission of CA Cdots remained stable providing a suitable ratiometric fluorescence signal of BPA detection. The proposed probe was successfully applied to the BPA detection in real water samples with high sensitivity and reliability and the results were compared with those obtained by HPLC with fluorescence detection (HPLC-FLD).

Tetrahydrofuran (THF, anhydrous ≥99.9 %, inhibitor-free), Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) polymers, and Bisphenol A (BPA, ≥99.9 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All other reagents were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was produced with a Millipore water purification system.

L was synthesized based on the procedure reported by D. Matt et al. [31], and functionalized Pdots were synthesized via the coprecipitation method. In the first step of, the stock solutions of PFBT (1 mg mL−1) and L (30 mM) in THF were prepared. Subsequently, 400 μL of PFBT and 10 μL of L from their stock solutions were dissolved in 1 mL THF, and the mixture was sonicated for 3 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Then, this homogeneous solution was quickly injected into 2 mL deionized water in a bath sonicator. The resulting solution was sonicated for 5 min to ensure that the prepared PFBT@L Pdots are well dispersed in the aqueous phase. Finally, the THF was evaporated at 60 °C by a stream of nitrogen gas, and resulting bright-yellow-emitting PFBT@L Pdots in the aqueous solution were obtained.

CA Cdots were synthesized by using a facile hydrothermal method, in which 3.8 g of CA and 2 g of urea were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Then the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, and the resultant brownish-yellow Cdots solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to eliminate the larger particles. The powder of Cdots was obtained by drying in the oven at 80 °C. Afterward, the stock solution of Cdots was prepared by dissolving Cdots into deionized water to obtain a final concentration of 300 μg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: For preparing the PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid, 200 μL of the PFBT@L Pdots (400 μg mL−1) was completely mixed with 100 μL of the Cdots solution (300 μg mL−1), and the mixed solution was incubated for 1 min at room temperature (25 °C). The resulting PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

In a typical measurement, the bulk solution of the PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid (300 μL) was first diluted by 10 mL of (10 mM, pH 8.0) phosphate buffer (PB) to achieve the final concentration of 11 μg mL−1 PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid (8 μg mL−1 of the PFBT@L Pdots and 3 μg mL−1 of the CA Cdots). For each measurement, 2 mL of the above solution was titrated with different concentrations of BPA, and fluorescent spectra were recorded over 380–760 nm under the excitation wavelength 340 nm and the excitation and emission slit widths 5 nm. The same procedure was applied to evaluate the influence of potential interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting BPA in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The bottled water was solely purchased from a local store. To be noted, the concentration of BPA in the bottled water sample was measured after the bottled water was heated to ∼80 °C. In a general procedure of BPA detection, a known quantity of BPA (200, 600, 1000, and 1500 nM) was spiked into the real water samples containing 8 μg mL−1 of the PFBT@L Pdots. At this time, fluorescence spectra were recorded by the luminescence spectrometer at excitation of 340 nm.

The fluorescence spectra were measured with a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp source. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with an Agilent 8453 Diode Array Spectrophotometer. An Agilent 1200 HPLC system with a fluorescence detector (HPLC-FLD) was used for Chromatographic measurements. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted on a Bruker infrared spectrophotometer, and a Metrohm pH Meter 713 was applied to read the pH values. In this work, each test parameter was accumulated in three repeated measurements, except for the real sample data, which every value is the average of five samples detected at the same conditions.

All of the computational models reported in this work were designed and geometrically optimized by Merck Molecular Force Field (MMFF94) method [32,33], in the Spartan ‘16 program suite [34]. Further, the single point calculation with the parameterized hybrid of ab-initio (restricted Hartree-Fock) and semi-empirical quantum mechanical (QM) method, so-called RHF/PM6 [35,36], was applied to confirm the binding mode and interacting energy in optimized models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of probe and material characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The PFBT@L Pdots were successfully synthesized by using PFBT polymer and L (Fig. 1b and c) as raw materials by simple co-precipitation method.

In this method, the highly fluorescent hydrophobic polymer (PFBT) is initially dissolved in a water-miscible organic solvent without any surfactant, followed by fast injection into water. The mechanism of the co-precipitation procedure is still unclear, presumably the drastic decrease in solubility and the hydrophobic interaction play an key role in the Pdots formation [19,23,37]. As mentioned, L was used to surface modification of Pdots. L is constructed with four arene units (calix [4]arene) which has been functionalized at the upper rim with the tert-butyl groups (t-But) and at the lower rim position with the CH2P(O)ph2, CH2CH2OMe, and chiral (R)-CH2C(O)NHCH(Me)Ph.

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX)spectroscopies is one of the most attractive technique to gain high spatial resolution elemental analysis [1,2]. Herein, elemental information of L was obtained through EDX spectroscopies (Figs. S1a and b). As expected, the EDX spectra revealed the presence of C, N, O, and P content, which have the satisfactory consistencies with the L compositions. Another technique which employ to determine the elemental composition of the materials and mass percentage composition for elements is CHN analysis. This technique is based on high temperature combustion (e.g. furnace temperature 1000 °C) in an oxygen-rich environment, which lead to convert C to CO2, H to H2O, and N to NO2 and remove them using various adsorbents [3]. Table S1 show the result of CHN elemental analyses for L, the results indicated the (wt%): C: 75.8, H: 7.5, N: 0.9, which consisted with the calculation results (C: 77, H: 6.7, N: 1) and elemental composition of L. Besides, 1HNMR spectrum of L in CDCl3 are shown peaks at: δ 9.42 (s, 1Hf, NH), 7.17–7.84 (25Hg, arom. H), 6.37–6.69 (m, 8Hh, m-H), 5.14 (m, 1Hi, NHCHMePh, 5.05 (s, 2Hj, PCH2), 4.95 (s, 2Hj, PCH2), 4.76–4.83 (d, 2Hk, OCH2– C(O), (J = 10 Hz), 4.37–4.67 (m, 8Hl, ArCH2), 3.63–3.73 (m, 2Hm, OCH2), 3.35–3.50 (m, 2Hm, OCH2), 2.93 (s, 3Hn, OCH3), 1.52–1.54 (d, 3Ho, NHCHMePh, J = 7.0 Hz, 0.9–1.1 (s, 36Hp, C(CH3)3 (, which was consistent with already report by D. Matt [31], Fig. S2a. Meanwhile, expanded regions of the 1H NMR spectra of L are given in Figs. S2b, c, and d. During PFBT@L Pdots formation, the hydrophobic t-But functional groups of L tended to anchor inside the core of the Pdots matrix, while the aromatic rings and their functional groups attached at the lower rim extended outside into the aqueous environment. To confirm the interaction between PFBT and L, the PFBT, L, and PFBT@L Pdots were further analyzed using 1H NMR. 1HNMR spectrum of PFBT@L Pdots revealed the resonances of the protons on the t-But groups of L shifted upfield by 0.03 ppm, while no noticeable changes were observed for the resonance of other protons in L. At the same time, the resonance of PFBT aromatic rings H d protons (at 8.03 ppm) and its octyl H e protons (at 0.8–2.1 ppm) undergo downfield shifted of 0.04 and 0.03 ppm, respectively, Fig. S3. This result was taken as evidence that L is attached to the surface of Pdots through hydrophobic interaction between the t-But functional groups of L and the Pdots matrix.

To analyze the optical properties of PFBT@L Pdots, the UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra of PFBT, L, and PFBT@L Pdots were recorded and are given in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. The UV–Vis absorption band of L was located at 207–275 nm, and PFBT exhibited a conspicuous absorption peak at 320 and 450 nm. According to previous studies, the peak at 207 and 320 are assigned to the π-π* transition of CC from aromatic sp2 domains and the n-π* transition of CO bonds [38]. Meanwhile, the absorption band at 450 nm is mainly generated by trapping of the excited surface state energy, which leads to the strong fluorescence emission at 540 nm. Interestingly, the absorption spectrum of the PFBT@L Pdots in water shows ≈20–30 nm red-shift compared to PFBT in THF, which resulted from the J aggregation of the PFBT polymer chain in the aqueous solution, and hydrophobic interaction between PFBT with L [39,40]. The fluorescence spectra of PFBT@L Pdots at different excitation wavelengths revealed an excitation-independent fluorescence feature with an optimum excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) was used to study the morphology of PFBT@L Pdots that exhibited good monodispersity and uniformity, with an average size of 50 nm, as presented in Fig. 2c. FT-IR analysis was carried out to characterize the surface composition and chemical state of materials. The FT-IR spectrum of L showed a characteristic peak at 1024 and 1116 cm−1 assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of (C–O), the strong peak at 1193 cm−1 related to υ (PO) of phosphine oxide. The characteristic peak at 1359, 1438, 1471, 1539, and 1672 cm−1 corresponded to the δ (CH3), υ (PCH2), υ (CC), δ (N–H), and υ (CO) respectively, Fig. 2d. All these peaks were also seen in the FT-IR spectrum of PFBT@L Pdots. Additionally, FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized PFBT@L Pdots exhibited a peak at 1622 cm−1 which corresponded to the δ (CN).

To explore the potential application of the PFBT@L Pdots for detection of BPA, the relevant experimental conditions, including pH of the solution, incubation time, the concentration of L, and probe dosage were optimized to improve the sensitivity of the fluorescence detecting method. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching was evaluated at different pH values by adjusting the pH of the PB solution over the range of 4.0–9.0. As the result shows in Fig. 3 a, neutral and alkaline pHs were more favorable than the acidic pHs, and pH 8.0 was found to be the optimum solution pH for BPA detection. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of PFBT@L Pdots under the addition of BPA was reached equilibrium after 60 s, so this time was chosen as optimum incubation time (Fig. 3b). Additionally, the influence of L concentration on the fluorescence response of Pdots toward BPA was also investigated, in which the PFBT@L Pdots with different L concentrations were prepared. As deposited in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence quenching of PFBT@L Pdots becomes more evident by increasing the concentration of L up to 0.4 mg, so this amount of L was used for the synthesis of Pdots. By investigating the fluorescence quenching percentage of Pdots at different concentrations but in the presence of a constant concentration of BPA, 8 mg mL−1 of Pdotd was selected as the optimum dosage for the probe concentration (Fig. 3d).

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence responses of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots to BPA at different concentrations were recorded. In the absence of BPA and under a single-excitation wavelength, the PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots emitted two well-resolved emission peaks at 440 and 542 nm, related to the fluorescence of CA Cdots and PFBT@L Pdots, respectively. Not surprisingly, by adding BPA into the solution of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid solution, the fluorescence intensity at 542 nm was continuously quenched, whereas the intensity at 440 nm remained unchanged (Fig. 4 a). As shown in Fig. 4b, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity was related to the amount of BPA in the concentration range of 50–2000 nM. The linear calibration curve was obtained from 50 to 1500 nM with a regression coefficient of 0.9963 and a detection limit of 8.5 nM based on the three times the standard deviation of the blank divided by the slope of a linear calibration curve. Owing to the self-calibration in the ratiometric fluorescence measurements, this method is more accurate than the single-intensity-based fluorescence method [41]. Moreover, in ratiometric fluorescence methods, fluorescence variations render the possibility of the visual emission color variations and simplicity of the visual detection of targets (the inset of Fig. 4a).

In this regard, a separate control experiment was conducted based on individual PFBT@L Pdots to compare ratiometric fluorescence method with the single-intensity-based method. The results show that upon the increment of BPA, the fluorescence intensity of PFBT@L Pdots at 440 nm was gradually decreased, Fig. 4c. It is obvious that the fluorescence of PFBT@L Pdots linearly quench by BPA in a concentration range of 50–1500 nM with a detection limit of 14.4 nM, Fig. 4d. In this case, monitoring the emission color of PFBT@L Pdots solution under the addition of BPA showed negligible color variations, and therefore, visual detection of analyte was almost impossible (inset of Fig. 4c).

The accuracy of a probe is assessed by its selectivity to the detection of the BPA. Thus, fluorescence response of the PFBT@L Pdots in the presence of possible interfering ions and compounds (1500 nM) that may be present in water samples was recorded (Fig. 5 ). The results showed that these interferents had almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the probe and indicated the high selectivity of the synthesized PFBT@L Pdots toward BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of the BPA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the response mechanism of the PFBT@L Pdots toward BPA, further control experiments were conducted. As a starting point, the fluorescence spectra of unfunctionalized PFBT Pdots in the presence of BPA were recorded. As shown in Fig. S4, upon adding different concentrations of BPA (up to 2000 nM) into unfunctionalized Pdots (PFBT Pdots) solution, the fluorescence intensity of PFBT Pdots almost remained unchanged. We also further interrogated the capability of the L for interaction with BPA. To examine this, emission scanning of L and BPA was recorded in the ethanol (L is insoluble in aqueous solvent), Figs. S5a and b. Upon the increment of L (from 0 to 1000 nM in ethanol) to BPA (750 M), the fluorescence intensity at 310 nm is gradually decreased as shown in Fig. S5c. However, the fluorescence intensity is unchanged at a concentration greater than 750 nM, which attests the feasible interaction of the L with BPA. The quantitative detection results were achieved by determining the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and L concentrations. A good linear correlation was observed ranging from 50 to 750 nM (Fig. S5d).

Therefore, it is suggested that fluorescence quenching of the PFBT@L Pdots in the presence of BPA results from the interaction between BPA and L on the surface of functionalized Pdots. Hence, 1HNMR analysis was employed to investigate the interaction between free L and BPA. Toward this end, a constant concentration of BPA (2 mM) was titrated with different concentrations of L (0.25–2 mM), as shown in Fig. S6a. 1H NMR titration demonstrated with increasing concentration of L, the intensity of peak at 8.5 ppm (which is corresponding to the resonances of protons on the hydroxyl groups H a ) gradually decreased with 0.09 ppm downfield shift, and finally in the 2 mM of L entirely disappearance. At the same time, the BPA aromatic H b protons (at 6.64–6.98 ppm) and its methyl H c protons (at 1.53 ppm) shifted downfield 0.02 ppm and upfield 0.03 ppm, respectively. Meanwhile, the resonances of protons on the lower rim of L including H f (at 9.42 ppm), H j (at 5.05 and 4.95 ppm), H i (at 5.14 ppm), and H p (at 0.9–1.1 ppm) shifted 0.08 downfield, 0.09 upfield, 0.03 downfield, 0.03 downfield, and 0.05 upfield, respectively, which resulted from the electrostatic interactions between L and BPA. 1HNMR titration results are evidence that the main interaction between BPA and L occurred through hydroxyl functional groups of BPA. These findings led us to propose that the oxygen lone-pair of BPA hydroxyl groups attack phosphorus atoms located at the lower rim of L, which led to the PO double bond breaking and P–O covalent single bond formation. Furthermore, this chemical bond formation is also evidenced in the FT-IR spectra, where revealed υ(PO) peak at 1195 cm−1 disappears, and a new P–O peak at 813 cm−1 appears (Fig. 2d). As well, 1HNMR spectra of BPA in the presence of PFBT@L Pdots demonstrated the disappearance of the proton resonances on the hydroxyl groups Ha peak (at 8.5 ppm), which was consistent with the 1H NMR titration results (Fig. S6b). Therefore, it can be suggested that the chemical bonding of the phosphorus atoms of neighboring PFBT@L Pdots with BPA hydroxyl functional groups leads to the aggregation of the PFBT@L Pdots. As a predicate to examine this suggestion, the PFBT@L Pdots size before and after adding BPA was determined by DLS measurement. As shown in Figs. S6c and d, the PFBT@L Pdots size was predominantly 50 nm and after adding BPA size of the particles reached to148.1 nm. All of these results taken together, led us to propose that aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (AIFQ) is the possible mechanism for this fluorescence quenching behavior in the PFBT@L Pdots.

In addition, theoretical calculations were also used for investigation of the interaction mechanism between the PFBT@L Pdots and L. The structures of L, BPA, and their different interaction modes were modeled and optimized by RHF/PM6 semi-empirical calculation method. Fig. 6 shows the structure of the optimized models in company with their molecular orbital energy diagrams. The optimized structures of L and BPA are shown in Fig. 6a and b. The interaction mode in L/BPA (Fig. 6c) is of host-guest type, signifying that there is no covalent bond between L and BPA. In Fig. 6d, one BPA molecule has lost two H atoms and was covalently bonded from both sides to the P atoms of one L molecule. In Fig. 6e, two BPA molecules were bonded to one L molecule, each by a single covalent P–O bond. Finally, in Fig. 6f, two BPA molecules were acted as bridges between two L molecules through four P–O covalent bonds. The length of the P–O bond in the structures of Fig. 6d–F was monitored. According to these results, the average P–O bond length in L-BPA was 2.74 Å, however, it was reduced to 1.88 Å for BPA-L-BPA and 1.79 Å for L-(BPA)2-L structures. This confirms the stability and higher possibility of the interaction mode between L and BPA in Fig. 6f. Furthermore, the length of a hydrogen bond between L and BPA in different interaction modes of Fig. 6c–F was also monitored in this study. The average H⋯O hydrogen bond in the host-guest interaction mode of L/BPA (Fig. 6c) was calculated to be 1.67 Å. Nevertheless, it was increased to 2.29 Å in BPA-L-BPA (Figs. 6e) and 2.06 Å in L-(BPA)2-L (Fig. 6f) structures. This also defines the higher possibility of the L-(BPA)2-L interaction mode compared to L-BPA or BPA-L-BPA. However, the stability (i.e. possibility) of the L/BPA is the highest among all mentioned cases. As well, Table 1 listed the energy value of the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO), and the bandgap (ΔEHOMO-LUMO) of the structures. It also gives the interaction energy (Eint) for the optimized models in the vacuum state. The interaction energy of each BPA with the L molecule was calculated using equation (1) [42]:
(1)
E
¬ int
AB
=
E AB
–
(
E A
+
E B
)
where, Eint AB, EAB, EA, and EB are the interaction energy of L and BPA, the total energy of BPA-interacted L, the energy of L, and the energy of BPA, respectively. Eventually, the results of the interaction energy calculations are in accordance with bond length monitoring, where the L/BPA has the highest negative energy of interaction (−64.03 kJ mol−1) among all other modes which is a strong hint for the highest stability and also probability of this mode among others. Besides, the L-(BPA)2-L structure with the interaction energy of −53.26 kJ mol−1 has the highest stability. L ligands are connected by bridging of BPA hydroxyl groups between them, where L phosphine groups act as a specific binding site to BPA. Theoretical calculation results match what was seen when BPA was added to the probe, where BPA caused the aggregation of the PFBT@L Pdots and turned off its fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BPA detection in the real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the actual application of this method, the potential application of PFBT@L Pdots for BPA detection in environmental samples, including bottled water was evaluated. Toward this end, the BPA content in the bottled water was measured by using the HPLC-FLD method. Furthermore, BPA was measured in the spiked bottled water samples using PFBT@L Pdots fluorescence probe and HPLC-FLD method, and the results were given in Table 2 . As can be seen, the satisfactory recovery rate for BPA was changed from 95.6 % to 103 %. The results indicated that the concentrations of BPA measured using the PFBT@L Pdots fluorescence probe were consistent with those obtained via the HPLC-FLD. In conclusion, the designed PFBT@L fluorescence probe could reliably and effectively detect BPA in actual water samples.

In the present work, we designed a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe for BPA detection based on PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid in which PFBT@L Pdots, which were Pdots modified with calixarene ligands, served as the detecting probe and CA Cdots acted as the internal reference. In the presence of BPA, calixarene ligands on Pdots surface were connected by bridging of BPA hydroxyl groups between them, which induced PFBT@L Pdots aggregation and fluorescence quenching. Under optimized conditions, the ratiometric signal of PFBT@L Pdots/CA Cdots hybrid demonstrated a linear response to BPA in the concentration range of 50–1500 nM and a suitable LOD as low as 8.5 nM. It was also found that the PFBT@L Pdots can be employed to BPA detection in the bottled water samples. More importantly, this is the first report that calixarene is used to functionalize Pdots and this can be extended to measure other analytes. In this perspective, Pdots with outstanding properties could be prepared by functionalizing their surface using other calixarenes with different functional groups on lower and upper rims for specific applications.

Sardar paydar: Investigation, Visualization, Resources, Data curation, Formal analysis. Foroozan Feizi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mojtaba Shamsipur: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Ali Barati: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software. Farimah Mousavi: Data curation, Methodology, Software. Dominique Matt: Data curation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Research Council of Razi University.
